description: Falls from changing tables, beds, and couches are among the most common infant injuries. Always keep one hand on baby.
actionSteps: Keep one hand on baby at all times during diaper changes
Have all supplies within arm's reach before starting
Use the floor for diaper changes if you need to step away

title: Practice Safe Babywearing (Mid Infant)
description: Baby carriers are convenient but must be used safely. The TICKS rule ensures safe positioning.
actionSteps: T-Tight, I-In view at all times, C-Close enough to kiss, K-Keep chin off chest, S-Supported back
Check that baby's face is visible and airways clear
Ensure carrier is appropriate for baby's age and weight

title: Track Developmental Milestones Without Comparing (Mid Infant)
description: Every baby develops at their own pace within a range. Focus on your baby's individual progress rather than comparing to others.
actionSteps: Use a milestone tracking app or pediatrician's checklist as a guide
Note what your baby is doing now rather than what they are not doing yet
Discuss any concerns at regular well-child visits

title: Provide 30 Minutes of Daily Tummy Time (Mid Infant)
description: Tummy time builds the neck, shoulder, and core muscles your baby needs for rolling, sitting, and crawling.
actionSteps: Start with short 1-2 minute sessions after diaper changes
Place interesting toys just out of reach to encourage lifting and reaching
Use your chest as a tummy time surface for face-to-face interaction

title: Know the Red Flags for Developmental Delays (Mid Infant)
description: While every child develops differently, certain signs warrant early evaluation. Early intervention leads to better outcomes.
actionSteps: Watch for no social smiling by 2 months or no head control by 4 months
Note if baby does not reach for objects by 5-6 months
Contact your pediatrician if you notice regression or loss of skills at any age

title: Support Visual Development with High-Contrast Items (Mid Infant)
description: Newborns see best in high contrast. Black and white patterns stimulate visual development in the first months.
actionSteps: Use high-contrast black and white cards or books for newborns
Place a safe unbreakable mirror near baby during tummy time
By 3-4 months, introduce brightly colored toys as color vision develops

title: Allow Free Movement for Physical Development (Mid Infant)
description: Babies need unrestricted time to move their arms and legs freely. Limit time in containers like car seats, swings, and bouncers.
actionSteps: Provide floor time on a clean safe surface multiple times daily
Limit time in any one container to 15-20 minutes
Dress baby in clothing that allows free movement of arms and legs

title: Respond to Your Baby's Cues to Build Brain Connections (Mid Infant)
description: Every time you respond to your baby's sounds, expressions, and movements, you strengthen neural connections in their developing brain.
actionSteps: When baby coos, coo back and pause for their response
Follow baby's gaze and talk about what they are looking at
Imitate baby's facial expressions and sounds during play

title: Maintain Regular Well-Child Checkups (Mid Infant)
description: Well-child visits include developmental screening, growth monitoring, and immunizations. Do not skip them even if baby seems healthy.
actionSteps: Follow the recommended schedule of visits in the first year
Bring a list of questions or concerns to each appointment
Ask about developmental screening at every visit

title: Understand That Development Is Not Linear (Mid Infant)
description: Babies may seem to stall or even regress slightly before a big leap. This is normal and does not indicate a problem.
actionSteps: Expect temporary sleep disruption or fussiness before new skills emerge
Do not panic if a skill seems to disappear temporarily
Focus on the overall trajectory of development over weeks and months

title: Follow the Recommended Immunization Schedule (Mid Infant)
description: Vaccines protect your baby from serious diseases. Follow the schedule recommended by your pediatrician, WHO, and CDC.
actionSteps: Keep a vaccination record and bring it to every appointment
Do not delay vaccines without medical reason as each delay increases vulnerability
Ask your pediatrician about any concerns rather than skipping vaccines

title: Wash Hands Before Handling Your Newborn (Mid Infant)
description: Newborns have immature immune systems. Hand hygiene is the simplest and most effective way to prevent infections.
actionSteps: Wash hands with soap and water for 20 seconds before touching baby
Ask all visitors to wash hands before holding your newborn
Use hand sanitizer when soap and water are not available

title: Know the Signs of Newborn Illness (Mid Infant)
description: Newborns can get sick quickly. Knowing warning signs helps you seek care promptly when it matters most.
actionSteps: Watch for fever above 100.4F or 38C in babies under 3 months as this is an emergency
Note persistent vomiting, refusal to feed, or unusual lethargy
Trust your instincts and call your pediatrician if something seems wrong

title: Attend All Scheduled Well-Child Visits (Mid Infant)
description: Well-child visits in the first year catch problems early. They include growth monitoring, developmental screening, and health guidance.
actionSteps: Follow the schedule: typically 1, 2, 4, 6, 9, and 12 months
Prepare questions about feeding, sleep, and development before each visit
Bring your vaccination record to every appointment

title: Practice Safe Cord Care for Newborns (Mid Infant)
description: Keep the umbilical cord stump clean and dry. It typically falls off within 1-3 weeks.
actionSteps: Keep the cord stump clean and dry, folding the diaper below it
Do not pull on the cord stump even if it seems loose
Contact your doctor if you see redness, swelling, or foul-smelling discharge

title: Protect Baby from Respiratory Infections (Mid Infant)
description: RSV and other respiratory viruses are dangerous for young infants. Limit exposure during the first months.
actionSteps: Limit visitors and avoid crowded places in the first 2 months
Keep anyone with cold symptoms away from your newborn
Ensure all caregivers are up to date on pertussis and flu vaccines

title: Use Vitamin D Drops for Breastfed Babies (Mid Infant)
description: Breast milk does not contain enough vitamin D. Breastfed babies need 400 IU of vitamin D daily starting in the first few days.
actionSteps: Give vitamin D drops daily as recommended by your pediatrician
Place drops on nipple before feeding or directly in baby's mouth
Continue supplementation until baby drinks enough fortified milk or formula

title: Learn Infant CPR Before You Need It (Mid Infant)
description: Knowing infant CPR can save your baby's life in a choking or breathing emergency. Take a class before or shortly after birth.
actionSteps: Take an accredited infant CPR class through Red Cross or a local hospital
Practice the techniques periodically to keep skills fresh
Ensure all regular caregivers are also CPR trained

title: Practice Skin-to-Skin Contact Daily (Mid Infant)
description: Skin-to-skin contact regulates baby's temperature, heart rate, and breathing while promoting bonding and breastfeeding success.
actionSteps: Hold baby against your bare chest for at least 30 minutes daily
Both parents can practice skin-to-skin contact
Use skin-to-skin to calm a fussy or overstimulated baby

title: Make Eye Contact During Feeding (Mid Infant)
description: Feeding is one of the most important bonding moments. Making eye contact during feeds strengthens attachment and makes baby feel secure.
actionSteps: Look into your baby's eyes during breast or bottle feeding
Talk softly or hum while feeding
Minimize distractions like phones during feeding times

title: Respond Promptly and Consistently to Your Baby (Mid Infant)
description: Consistent responsive caregiving builds secure attachment, which is the foundation for all future relationships.
actionSteps: Pick up baby when they cry within a reasonable time
Respond to smiles, coos, and reaching out with warmth
Be the steady presence your baby can count on

title: Talk and Sing to Your Baby Throughout the Day (Mid Infant)
description: Your voice is your baby's favorite sound. Narrating your day and singing builds language connections and strengthens your bond.
actionSteps: Describe what you are doing during diaper changes, cooking, and walks
Sing lullabies, nursery rhymes, or any songs you enjoy
Use a warm expressive voice and watch for baby's response

title: Include Both Parents in Daily Care Routines (Mid Infant)
description: When both parents are involved in caregiving from the start, babies form secure attachments with both and parents feel more confident.
actionSteps: Share feeding, diaper changing, and bath time responsibilities
Give the non-birthing parent dedicated solo care time with baby
Support each other and avoid gatekeeping parenting tasks

title: Read to Your Baby from Birth (Mid Infant)
description: It is never too early to read to your baby. Reading promotes bonding and exposes babies to language patterns from the very start.
actionSteps: Choose high-contrast board books for newborns
Hold baby close while reading and use an animated voice
Make reading a part of the daily routine even if sessions are short

title: Create Calm Connection Moments (Mid Infant)
description: In the busy newborn period, find moments of quiet connection where you simply hold and look at your baby without an agenda.
actionSteps: Take a few minutes each day to just hold and gaze at your baby
Put away your phone during these connection moments
Follow baby's lead and match their pace

title: Use Gentle Touch and Infant Massage (Mid Infant)
description: Gentle massage can calm a fussy baby, improve sleep, and strengthen the parent-child bond through positive touch.
actionSteps: Use gentle strokes on baby's legs, arms, and back during calm alert times
Watch baby's cues and stop if they seem uncomfortable
Make massage a calming part of the bedtime routine

title: Start Tummy Time from Day One (Mid Infant)
description: Tummy time builds the muscles needed for head control, rolling, sitting, and crawling. Start with short sessions immediately.
actionSteps: Begin with 1-2 minutes several times a day on a firm surface
Place yourself face-to-face with baby to encourage head lifting
Gradually increase duration as baby gets stronger, aiming for 30 minutes daily by 3 months

title: Encourage Reaching and Grasping (Mid Infant)
description: By 3-4 months, babies start reaching for objects. Providing opportunities to practice develops hand-eye coordination and fine motor skills.
actionSteps: Hold colorful toys within reaching distance during play time
Offer lightweight rattles that are easy to grip
Let baby bring objects to their mouth as this is how they explore

title: Vary Baby's Positions Throughout the Day (Mid Infant)
description: Different positions build different muscle groups. Alternate between tummy time, back time, side-lying, and supported sitting.
actionSteps: Switch positions every 15-20 minutes during awake time
Use supported sitting in your lap to build core strength
Try side-lying with a rolled towel behind baby to encourage rolling

title: Use Toys Just Out of Reach to Motivate Movement (Mid Infant)
description: Placing interesting items slightly beyond your baby's grasp encourages stretching, pivoting, and eventually crawling.
actionSteps: During tummy time, place a favorite toy just beyond arm's reach
Move the toy slowly to encourage tracking and reaching
Celebrate any attempts to move toward the object

title: Allow Free Arm and Leg Movement (Mid Infant)
description: Babies need unrestricted time to kick, wave, and move freely. This develops muscle strength and body awareness.
actionSteps: Give floor time on a play mat without restricting clothing
Limit time in swings, bouncers, and car seats when not traveling
Let baby be in just a diaper for free movement in warm temperatures

title: Support Pre-Rolling Skills (Mid Infant)
description: Before rolling over, babies practice weight shifting and turning to one side. You can support this development through positioning.
actionSteps: Offer toys to one side to encourage head turning and weight shifting
Gently help baby experience rolling by guiding them from back to side
Practice both directions to develop muscles evenly

title: Introduce Safe Textured Objects for Grasping (Mid Infant)
description: Different textures help develop tactile awareness and encourage fine motor exploration.
actionSteps: Offer soft fabric toys, smooth wooden rings, and textured rubber toys
Let baby explore different materials during supervised play
Ensure all objects are large enough to prevent choking

title: Do Gentle Leg Exercises During Diaper Changes (Mid Infant)
description: Diaper changes are a perfect time for gentle motor play. Bicycle legs and gentle stretches support physical development.
actionSteps: Gently bicycle baby's legs during diaper changes
Practice gentle leg straightening and bending movements
Sing a song while doing the movements to make it fun and predictable

title: Talk to Your Baby Constantly (Mid Infant)
description: Your baby learns language by hearing it. Narrate your activities, describe what you see, and talk to your baby throughout the day.
actionSteps: Describe what you are doing during daily routines like diaper changes and feeding
Name objects, people, and actions as you encounter them
Use a warm expressive tone that naturally captures baby's attention

title: Respond to Babbling as Real Conversation (Mid Infant)
description: When your baby coos, babbles, or makes sounds, respond as if they said something meaningful. This serve-and-return builds language.
actionSteps: When baby makes a sound, pause and respond with interest
Copy their sounds back to them and wait for a reply
Take turns making sounds like a real conversation

title: Read Board Books Daily from Birth (Mid Infant)
description: Reading to your baby from birth exposes them to rich language patterns and builds the habit that will support literacy.
actionSteps: Choose simple high-contrast books for newborns and colorful board books for older babies
Point to pictures and name them
Make reading part of the daily routine especially at bedtime

title: Sing Nursery Rhymes and Songs (Mid Infant)
description: Songs and rhymes teach language patterns, rhythm, and memory. The repetition in songs helps babies learn sounds and words.
actionSteps: Sing the same songs repeatedly as repetition aids learning
Add hand motions and gestures to songs like Itsy Bitsy Spider
Sing during routine activities like diaper changes and car rides

title: Use Your Baby's Name Frequently (Mid Infant)
description: Hearing their name helps babies develop identity and learn to pay attention when spoken to.
actionSteps: Call your baby by name when talking to them or approaching them
Use their name before giving information to get their attention
Respond when baby turns toward their name with a smile or interaction

title: Look Into Baby's Eyes During Communication (Mid Infant)
description: Babies learn language partly by watching mouth movements and facial expressions. Face-to-face interaction is essential.
actionSteps: Position yourself at baby's eye level during talking and reading
Make exaggerated facial expressions while speaking
Allow baby to watch your mouth as you form words

title: Point at Objects and Name Them (Mid Infant)
description: Pointing and naming objects teaches your baby to connect words with things. Joint attention is a critical language milestone.
actionSteps: Point to objects throughout the day and say their names
Follow baby's gaze and name what they are looking at
When baby points, name the object they are pointing at enthusiastically

title: Expose Baby to Rich Vocabulary (Mid Infant)
description: Use a variety of words, not just simplified baby talk. Babies who hear more diverse vocabulary develop larger vocabularies.
actionSteps: Describe colors, sizes, textures, and feelings in your narration
Use real names for objects rather than only baby words
Read books with varied vocabulary appropriate for your enjoyment

title: Play Cause-and-Effect Games (Mid Infant)
description: Babies learn about cause and effect through simple actions. Rattles, squeaky toys, and dropping games teach that actions have results.
actionSteps: Offer rattles and toys that make sounds when shaken or squeezed
Play with pop-up toys and push-button cause-effect toys
Let baby drop objects from the high chair and hand them back

title: Support Object Permanence with Peek-a-Boo (Mid Infant)
description: Object permanence, understanding that things exist even when hidden, develops around 4-8 months. Peek-a-boo is the perfect game.
actionSteps: Play peek-a-boo with your hands, blankets, or by hiding behind furniture
Partially hide toys under a blanket and let baby find them
Progress to fully hiding objects as understanding develops

title: Provide Varied Sensory Experiences (Mid Infant)
description: Rich sensory input builds neural pathways. Expose your baby to different textures, sounds, sights, and safe tastes.
actionSteps: Offer toys with different textures like smooth, bumpy, soft, and crinkly
Take baby outside to experience wind, sunlight, and nature sounds
Let baby safely explore food textures during early solid introduction

title: Offer Age-Appropriate Toys for Exploration (Mid Infant)
description: Simple toys that can be manipulated in many ways are better for cognitive development than complex electronic ones.
actionSteps: Choose rattles, soft blocks, textured balls, and safe household items
Rotate toys weekly to maintain novelty and engagement
Avoid electronic toys that do all the work for baby

title: Follow Baby's Gaze and Interest (Mid Infant)
description: When you notice what captures your baby's attention and join them in exploring it, you support their natural learning drive.
actionSteps: Watch what your baby looks at and talk about it
Bring objects closer that they seem interested in
Let baby explore at their own pace without rushing to the next thing

title: Introduce Safe Mirrors for Self-Recognition (Mid Infant)
description: Babies are fascinated by faces including their own. Safe mirrors support self-awareness and social cognitive development.
actionSteps: Place a baby-safe unbreakable mirror at floor level for tummy time
Hold baby in front of a mirror and point out features
Wave and make faces together in the mirror

title: Create Contrast Between Calm and Active Times (Mid Infant)
description: Alternating between stimulating play and calm rest teaches babies to regulate arousal levels and process experiences.
actionSteps: Follow active play sessions with calm activities like reading or cuddling
Watch for overstimulation signs and transition to calmer activities
Create a rhythm of activity and rest throughout the day

title: Talk Through Daily Routines to Build Understanding (Mid Infant)
description: Narrating routine activities helps babies begin to understand sequences and anticipate what comes next.
actionSteps: Describe each step during diaper changes, baths, and feedings
Use consistent phrases for routine beginnings like time for your bath
Pause and let baby anticipate the next familiar step

title: Smile and Respond to Your Baby's Social Cues (Mid Infant)
description: Social smiling, cooing, and reaching out are your baby's first social behaviors. Responding warmly teaches them about relationships.
actionSteps: Smile back when your baby smiles at you
Engage enthusiastically when they reach out or coo
Create back-and-forth exchanges of sounds and expressions

title: Introduce Your Baby to Different People Gently (Mid Infant)
description: Exposure to different faces and voices builds social awareness. Go at baby's pace and do not force interactions.
actionSteps: Allow brief visits with trusted family members and friends
Hold baby during introductions to new people for security
Respect baby's cues if they become overwhelmed or shy

title: Play Face-to-Face Social Games (Mid Infant)
description: Games like peek-a-boo and pat-a-cake teach turn-taking, anticipation, and social engagement in the earliest months.
actionSteps: Play peek-a-boo using your hands or a cloth multiple times daily
Practice pat-a-cake and other clapping games
Imitate your baby's facial expressions and sounds

title: Describe Other People's Emotions (Mid Infant)
description: Even babies benefit from hearing about emotions. Narrating feelings you observe helps develop early emotional awareness.
actionSteps: Point out expressions saying look, that baby is smiling, they are happy
Name your own feelings throughout the day
Use a warm expressive voice that matches the emotion you describe

title: Encourage Interactions with Siblings (Mid Infant)
description: If you have older children, supervised interaction benefits both the baby and sibling. Guide gentle appropriate contact.
actionSteps: Show older siblings how to interact gently with the baby
Praise kind and gentle behavior toward the baby
Supervise all interactions and teach that baby is fragile

title: Provide Opportunities for Observing Other Babies (Mid Infant)
description: Even before interactive play, babies enjoy watching other babies. This early observation is the beginning of social interest.
actionSteps: Attend baby groups or meet with other families with infants
Let babies observe each other at a comfortable distance
Do not force interaction but allow natural curiosity

title: Model Warm Social Interactions (Mid Infant)
description: Your baby watches how you interact with others. Modeling kindness, greeting people, and showing affection teaches social norms.
actionSteps: Greet people warmly when baby is present
Show affection to your partner, family members, and friends
Say please and thank you in baby's presence consistently

title: Respond to Stranger Anxiety with Support (Mid Infant)
description: Stranger anxiety appears around 6-9 months and is a normal developmental milestone. Support your baby through it.
actionSteps: Stay close and calm when baby encounters unfamiliar people
Allow baby to warm up at their own pace from the safety of your arms
Tell others your baby is going through a normal shy phase without apologizing

title: Establish a Consistent Bedtime Routine (Late Infant)
description: A predictable 20-30 minute wind-down routine signals to your toddler that sleep is coming. Consistency is more important than the specific activities.
actionSteps: Choose 3-4 calming activities in the same order each night
Include a bath, pajamas, book reading, and a song or cuddle
Start the routine at the same time each evening

title: Navigate the Two-to-One Nap Transition (Late Infant)
description: Between 12-18 months, most toddlers drop to one midday nap. Watch for signs like resisting the morning nap or taking very short naps.
actionSteps: Gradually push the morning nap later by 15-30 minutes
Offer an earlier bedtime during the transition period
Expect some fussy afternoons for 2-4 weeks during adjustment

title: Handle Bedtime Resistance with Calm Consistency (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers often test boundaries at bedtime by asking for extra drinks, stories, or hugs. Acknowledge their feelings but hold the boundary.
actionSteps: Offer one final choice before lights out (which book or which stuffed animal)
Use a calm firm voice to say goodnight and leave the room
If they get up, silently return them to bed without engaging

title: Ensure 11-14 Hours of Total Sleep Daily (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers aged 12-24 months need 11-14 hours of sleep including naps. Consistent insufficient sleep affects mood, learning, and growth.
actionSteps: Track total sleep including naps over a week to spot patterns
Adjust bedtime earlier if your toddler seems chronically overtired
Protect nap time as a non-negotiable part of the daily schedule

title: Create a Sleep-Friendly Toddler Room (Late Infant)
description: As your child transitions from crib to toddler bed, ensure the room is safe and conducive to sleep.
actionSteps: Childproof the room so your toddler is safe if they get out of bed
Remove screens and stimulating toys from the sleep area
Use a dim nightlight if your child is afraid of the dark

title: Manage Separation Anxiety at Bedtime (Late Infant)
description: Separation anxiety peaks around 8-18 months. Your child may cry when you leave the room at bedtime, which is developmentally normal.
actionSteps: Practice short separations during the day to build confidence
Create a brief consistent goodbye ritual at bedtime
Reassure your child you will be nearby but avoid prolonged check-ins

title: Eliminate Screens Before Bedtime (Late Infant)
description: Screen exposure within 1-2 hours of bedtime delays melatonin production and makes it harder for toddlers to fall asleep.
actionSteps: Turn off all screens at least one hour before bedtime
Replace screen time with books, puzzles, or quiet play
Keep tablets and phones out of the bedroom entirely

title: Address Night Waking in Toddlers (Late Infant)
description: Occasional night waking is normal but frequent waking may indicate overtiredness, hunger, or environmental disruption.
actionSteps: Keep the response brief and boring to avoid reinforcing waking
Check for teething, illness, or room temperature issues
Ensure your child is getting enough daytime calories and activity

title: Introduce Solids at Six Months with Iron-Rich Foods (Late Infant)
description: At 6 months, start complementary foods alongside continued breastfeeding. Iron-rich foods are critical as iron stores from birth begin to deplete.
actionSteps: Start with iron-fortified cereal, pureed meat, or mashed lentils
Introduce one new single-ingredient food every 3-5 days
Continue breastfeeding alongside solids through 12 months and beyond

title: Progress Textures by 10 Months to Prevent Feeding Difficulties (Late Infant)
description: WHO research shows that delaying lumpy foods past 10 months increases the risk of feeding difficulties later. Progress textures gradually.
actionSteps: Move from purees to mashed foods with soft lumps by 8-9 months
Offer safe finger foods like soft cooked vegetables and ripe fruit
By 10 months, include finely chopped family foods

title: Cut Foods Safely to Prevent Choking (Late Infant)
description: Certain food shapes and sizes are choking hazards for young children. Always cut round foods lengthwise and avoid hard small items.
actionSteps: Quarter grapes and cherry tomatoes lengthwise
Cut hot dogs into thin strips, never round coins
Cook carrots and apples until soft before serving

title: Introduce Common Allergens from Six Months (Late Infant)
description: Current evidence shows no benefit to delaying allergens. Introduce peanut, egg, dairy, wheat, fish, and soy from 6 months.
actionSteps: Start with thin peanut butter mixed with water or breast milk
Introduce one new allergen at a time and wait 3-5 days
Continue offering allergens regularly once introduced without reaction

title: Avoid Honey Before 12 Months (Late Infant)
description: Honey can contain botulism spores that are dangerous for babies under one year. Their digestive system cannot neutralize the toxin.
actionSteps: Check ingredient labels for hidden honey in foods and drinks
Do not use honey as a sweetener in homemade baby food
After 12 months, honey is safe to introduce in small amounts

title: Practice Responsive Feeding with Eye Contact (Late Infant)
description: Feed your toddler directly with patience. Make eye contact, go at their pace, and never force food. This builds healthy eating habits.
actionSteps: Sit face-to-face during meals and make eye contact
Offer food and wait for your child to open their mouth willingly
Stop feeding when they turn away or show disinterest

title: Offer 3-4 Meals Plus Snacks for Toddlers (Late Infant)
description: Between 12-24 months, toddlers need 3-4 meals and 1-2 nutritious snacks daily. Small stomachs need frequent refueling.
actionSteps: Offer meals and snacks at regular times to establish rhythm
Include a variety of food groups at each meal
Keep snacks nutritious rather than using them as treats or pacifiers

title: Let Your Toddler Self-Feed to Build Independence (Late Infant)
description: Messy self-feeding is an important developmental step. It builds fine motor skills and gives your child autonomy over eating.
actionSteps: Offer finger foods they can pick up independently
Provide a child-sized spoon and let them practice even if messy
Put a mat under the high chair and accept the mess as part of learning

title: Understand That Tantrums Are Developmentally Normal (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers have big feelings but limited vocabulary and impulse control. Tantrums are how they express frustration, not manipulation.
actionSteps: Stay calm and present during the tantrum without giving in or punishing
Acknowledge their feeling by saying something like you seem really frustrated
Wait for the storm to pass then offer comfort and move on

title: Offer Two Choices Instead of Saying No (Late Infant)
description: Giving your toddler choices between two acceptable options reduces power struggles and builds decision-making skills.
actionSteps: Instead of do you want to get dressed ask red shirt or blue shirt
Offer choices you are comfortable with either outcome
Limit to two options to avoid overwhelming your toddler

title: Use Positive Language to Guide Behavior (Late Infant)
description: Tell your toddler what to do rather than what not to do. Positive instructions are easier for young brains to process.
actionSteps: Say walk please instead of don't run
Say gentle hands instead of don't hit
Say food stays on the table instead of don't throw food

title: Stay Calm During Your Toddler's Big Emotions (Late Infant)
description: Your toddler co-regulates through you. When you stay calm, you model emotional regulation and help them feel safe.
actionSteps: Take a deep breath before responding to challenging behavior
Use a low calm voice even when your child is screaming
Remove yourself briefly if you feel your own anger rising

title: Prepare Your Toddler for Transitions (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers struggle with sudden changes. Giving warnings before transitions reduces meltdowns.
actionSteps: Give a 5-minute and 2-minute warning before ending an activity
Use a timer or song to make the transition predictable
Describe what is coming next to help them mentally prepare

title: Catch Your Toddler Being Good (Late Infant)
description: Attention is the most powerful motivator for toddlers. Praising positive behavior increases it more than punishing negative behavior.
actionSteps: Specifically name the good behavior you see
Say things like I love how you shared that toy with your sister
Give attention during calm moments not just during misbehavior

title: Set Simple Clear Boundaries Consistently (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers feel secure when they know the rules. Consistent boundaries with warm enforcement build cooperation over time.
actionSteps: Choose a few important non-negotiable rules and enforce them every time
Use short clear statements like we sit at the table to eat
Follow through calmly with the same consequence each time

title: Avoid Shaming or Harsh Discipline (Late Infant)
description: Shaming damages self-esteem and does not teach better behavior. Gentle correction teaches while preserving your child's dignity.
actionSteps: Correct the behavior not the child by saying hitting hurts instead of you are bad
Avoid yelling or threatening as primary discipline tools
Use natural consequences when safe and appropriate

title: Childproof Your Home Before Your Baby Crawls (Late Infant)
description: Childproofing should happen before your baby becomes mobile. Cover outlets, secure furniture, and lock cabinets with hazardous items.
actionSteps: Install outlet covers, cabinet locks, and furniture anchors
Gate the top and bottom of stairs
Get on hands and knees to identify hazards at your child's level

title: Lock Away All Medications and Cleaning Products (Late Infant)
description: Poisoning is a leading cause of injury in toddlers. All medications and chemicals must be in locked cabinets, not just out of reach.
actionSteps: Use child-resistant locks on cabinets with medications and cleaners
Store items in original containers with labels intact
Save poison control number in your phone

title: Never Leave Your Child Alone Near Water (Late Infant)
description: Drowning can happen in as little as one inch of water in under two minutes. Constant supervision near any water source is essential.
actionSteps: Stay within arm's reach during bath time, every single time
Empty buckets, kiddie pools, and containers immediately after use
Install pool fencing with self-closing gates if you have a pool

title: Secure Heavy Furniture and TVs to Walls (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers climb on everything. Unsecured furniture and televisions tip over, causing serious injuries and deaths every year.
actionSteps: Anchor bookshelves, dressers, and TV stands to wall studs
Place heavy items on lower shelves to lower center of gravity
Remove items from furniture tops that might tempt climbing

title: Teach Basic Water Safety Early (Late Infant)
description: Even before swimming lessons, teach your toddler to never approach water without an adult and to wait for permission.
actionSteps: Teach your child to always ask an adult before going near water
Practice sitting on pool or tub edges, not standing
Consider infant survival swimming lessons starting at 6 months

title: Use Window Guards and Stops (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers can fall through screens. Install window guards or stops that prevent windows from opening more than 4 inches.
actionSteps: Install window guards on all windows above ground floor
Move furniture away from windows to prevent climbing access
Open windows from the top rather than the bottom when possible

title: Keep Button Batteries Away from Children (Late Infant)
description: Button batteries can cause fatal internal burns if swallowed. They are found in remotes, toys, watches, and hearing aids.
actionSteps: Tape battery compartments shut on devices within reach
Dispose of used batteries immediately and securely
Seek emergency care immediately if you suspect battery ingestion

title: Apply Sunscreen and Protect from Sun Exposure (Late Infant)
description: Toddler skin is sensitive to UV damage. Use sunscreen, hats, and shade to prevent sunburn, which increases skin cancer risk later.
actionSteps: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen 15 minutes before going outside
Reapply every 2 hours and after water or sweating
Use wide-brimmed hats, sunglasses, and UPF clothing for extra protection

title: Ensure 180 Minutes of Physical Activity Daily (Late Infant)
description: WHO recommends at least 3 hours of physical activity per day for toddlers, including active play, walking, and climbing.
actionSteps: Break activity into multiple sessions throughout the day
Include outdoor play whenever weather permits
Provide safe spaces for crawling, walking, climbing, and running

title: Limit Screen Time Appropriately for Age (Late Infant)
description: No screen time before 12 months, and no more than 1 hour per day from ages 2-5. Screen time displaces active play and social interaction.
actionSteps: Avoid screens entirely for children under 12 months
For ages 1-2, choose high-quality educational content only if any
Co-view and discuss content rather than using screens as a babysitter

title: Encourage Problem-Solving Before Helping (Late Infant)
description: When your toddler struggles with a task, wait and observe before stepping in. Productive struggle builds problem-solving skills.
actionSteps: Count to 10 internally before offering help
Offer hints or partial help rather than doing it for them
Celebrate their persistence regardless of the outcome

title: Create a Yes-Space for Safe Exploration (Late Infant)
description: Designate an area where everything is safe for your toddler to explore freely. This reduces the need for constant no.
actionSteps: Childproof one room or area completely so everything is safe to touch
Rotate interesting and age-appropriate items in this space
Allow free exploration without hovering or restricting

title: Watch for Regression After Big Changes (Late Infant)
description: Developmental regression (loss of previously acquired skills) after events like a new sibling or move is usually temporary.
actionSteps: Maintain routines as much as possible during transitions
Offer extra comfort and patience during regression periods
Contact your pediatrician if regression lasts more than a few weeks or seems unrelated to life changes

title: Encourage Imitation and Pretend Play (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers learn through imitation. Pretend play like feeding a doll or talking on a toy phone develops cognitive and social skills.
actionSteps: Provide simple pretend play props like toy phones, dolls, and play food
Join in their pretend play and follow their lead
Model everyday activities like cooking and cleaning for them to copy

title: Know When to Seek Developmental Evaluation (Late Infant)
description: Trust your instincts as a parent. If something feels off about your child's development, early evaluation is always better than waiting.
actionSteps: Seek evaluation if your child is not walking by 18 months
Get assessed if there are no words by 16 months or no two-word phrases by 24 months
Do not let anyone dismiss your concerns with they will grow out of it without proper screening

title: Support Development Through Outdoor Nature Play (Late Infant)
description: Nature provides rich sensory experiences that support development. Outdoor time improves motor skills, attention, and mood.
actionSteps: Visit parks, gardens, and natural areas regularly
Let your child explore grass, sand, water, leaves, and sticks
Name plants, animals, insects, and natural phenomena you encounter together

title: Begin Dental Care with the First Tooth (Late Infant)
description: Start brushing as soon as the first tooth appears. Early dental care prevents cavities and establishes lifelong habits.
actionSteps: Use a smear of fluoride toothpaste on a soft infant toothbrush
Brush twice daily, especially before bed
Schedule the first dental visit by age 1 or within 6 months of the first tooth

title: Teach Hand Washing as a Habit (Late Infant)
description: Hand washing is the best defense against illness. Make it a routine part of your toddler's day.
actionSteps: Wash hands before meals, after toileting, and after playing outside
Sing a 20-second song to ensure thorough washing
Use a step stool so your child can reach the sink independently

title: Provide Fluoride Through Tap Water (Late Infant)
description: Fluoridated water strengthens developing teeth and prevents cavities. If your water is not fluoridated, ask your dentist about supplements.
actionSteps: Use tap water for drinking if it is fluoridated in your area
Limit juice to half a cup per day and avoid sugary beverages
Check with your dentist about fluoride varnish at regular visits

title: Keep Immunizations Up to Date Through Toddler Years (Late Infant)
description: The vaccination schedule continues through toddlerhood. Timely boosters maintain protection against preventable diseases.
actionSteps: Follow the recommended 12, 15, and 18 month vaccine schedules
Keep your child's immunization record updated and accessible
Ask about any missed or delayed vaccines at every visit

title: Recognize Signs of Common Childhood Illnesses (Late Infant)
description: Learn to identify ear infections, hand-foot-mouth disease, croup, and other common toddler illnesses to seek appropriate care.
actionSteps: Watch for ear pulling with fever as a sign of ear infection
Know that a barking cough at night may be croup
Call your doctor for persistent high fever, difficulty breathing, or signs of dehydration

title: Ensure Adequate Iron Intake After 12 Months (Late Infant)
description: Iron deficiency is common in toddlers. After transitioning from formula or continued breastfeeding, ensure iron-rich food sources.
actionSteps: Offer iron-rich foods like lean meats, beans, fortified cereals, and spinach
Pair iron-rich foods with vitamin C sources for better absorption
Limit cow's milk to 16-24 ounces per day as excess milk decreases iron absorption

title: Establish a Regular Sleep Schedule for Immune Health (Late Infant)
description: Consistent adequate sleep strengthens your toddler's immune system. Sleep-deprived children get sick more often.
actionSteps: Maintain a consistent bedtime and wake time
Ensure total sleep meets the 11-14 hour recommendation
Prioritize sleep during illness with earlier bedtimes

title: Schedule Regular Well-Child Visits Through Toddlerhood (Late Infant)
description: Continue well-child visits at 12, 15, 18, and 24 months. These visits track growth, development, and catch emerging health issues.
actionSteps: Do not skip visits even if your child seems healthy
Bring a written list of developmental questions or concerns
Ask about lead screening, vision, and hearing at appropriate ages

title: Follow Your Child's Lead in Play (Late Infant)
description: When you let your toddler direct play, they feel valued and in control. This builds confidence and deepens your connection.
actionSteps: Sit at their level and wait for them to invite you into play
Narrate what they are doing instead of directing the activity
Resist the urge to teach or correct during child-led play time

title: Praise New Skill Attempts Enthusiastically (Late Infant)
description: When your toddler tries something new, your encouragement matters more than their success. Celebrate the attempt.
actionSteps: Cheer for attempts even when they do not succeed
Use specific praise like you tried so hard to stack those blocks
Avoid only praising results or comparing to other children

title: Read Together Every Day (Late Infant)
description: Daily reading is the single most impactful activity for your child's development. It builds vocabulary, comprehension, and closeness.
actionSteps: Read at least one book together every day making it a non-negotiable routine
Let your child choose the book even if it is the same one again
Ask questions about the pictures and let them turn the pages

title: Create Special One-on-One Time (Late Infant)
description: Dedicated time with each child, even 10-15 minutes of focused attention, strengthens your relationship more than hours of distracted togetherness.
actionSteps: Set aside at least 10 minutes daily of phone-free child-focused time
Let your child choose the activity during this special time
Call it by a special name like our special time to make it feel important

title: Use Physical Affection Generously (Late Infant)
description: Hugs, cuddles, and physical closeness release oxytocin in both parent and child, strengthening the bond and reducing stress.
actionSteps: Offer hugs, lap sitting, and cuddles throughout the day
Respect when your child pulls away and do not force physical affection
Create a goodnight ritual that includes physical connection like a hug and a kiss

title: Let Your Toddler Help with Household Tasks (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers love to imitate grown-up activities. Including them in chores builds competence, connection, and a sense of belonging.
actionSteps: Give them a small cloth to wipe surfaces while you clean
Let them help stir, pour, or wash vegetables during cooking
Thank them for their help and tell them they are an important part of the family

title: Expand Your Toddler's Words with Conversation (Late Infant)
description: When your toddler says a word, expand it into a full sentence. This builds language while showing you listen and care about what they say.
actionSteps: If they say ball, respond with yes, it is a big red ball
Add new information to their observations like the dog is running fast
Pause and give them time to respond even if it takes a while

title: Be Present During Transitions and Hard Moments (Late Infant)
description: Your calm presence during difficult moments like goodbyes, doctor visits, or frustrations teaches your child they can count on you.
actionSteps: Stay close and offer comfort during hard transitions like daycare drop-off
Acknowledge their feelings with I know this is hard
Avoid sneaking away or dismissing their distress

title: Support Cruising and Early Walking Safely (Late Infant)
description: When your baby starts pulling up and cruising along furniture, ensure the environment supports safe practice.
actionSteps: Arrange sturdy furniture for cruising with no sharp corners
Clear the floor of tripping hazards
Offer push toys that provide stability for walking practice

title: Encourage Climbing in Safe Environments (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers are natural climbers. Rather than stopping all climbing, provide safe opportunities to develop this important skill.
actionSteps: Set up safe climbing opportunities with cushions, low steps, or toddler climbers
Supervise closely and stay nearby to spot them
Teach them to climb down feet first from furniture and stairs

title: Practice Stacking, Sorting, and Building (Late Infant)
description: Stacking blocks and sorting objects develops fine motor control, hand-eye coordination, and early math concepts.
actionSteps: Start with large soft blocks and progress to smaller ones
Demonstrate stacking and let them try and knock them down
Offer containers of different sizes for nesting and sorting

title: Encourage Ball Play for Coordination (Late Infant)
description: Rolling, throwing, and kicking balls develops gross motor skills, coordination, and teaches cause and effect.
actionSteps: Start with sitting face-to-face and rolling a ball back and forth
Progress to throwing and catching with a large soft ball
Introduce gentle kicking once your child is walking steadily

title: Introduce Crayons and Scribbling (Late Infant)
description: Scribbling is the first step toward writing. It develops hand strength, grasp patterns, and creative expression.
actionSteps: Offer large chunky crayons or triangular crayons for easier grip
Tape paper to the table or use a large surface like an easel
Model scribbling and drawing simple shapes without requiring imitation

title: Practice Going Up and Down Stairs (Late Infant)
description: Stair climbing develops leg strength, balance, and coordination. Always supervise and teach proper technique.
actionSteps: Start with crawling up stairs and scooting down on their bottom
Progress to walking up holding the railing or your hand
Teach them to turn around and go down feet first

title: Use Containers for Put-In Take-Out Play (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers are fascinated by putting objects in containers and dumping them out. This simple play builds fine motor skills and cognitive concepts.
actionSteps: Provide containers with lids, boxes, and buckets
Offer objects of various sizes to put in and take out
Demonstrate opening, filling, dumping, and closing

title: Offer Push and Pull Toys for Walking Confidence (Late Infant)
description: Push toys provide stability for new walkers. Pull toys encourage confident walking while looking backward.
actionSteps: Start with a sturdy push walker for early walkers
Introduce pull toys once walking is steady and confident
Choose toys with wheels that roll smoothly on your floor surface

title: Expand Your Toddler's Single Words into Sentences (Late Infant)
description: When your toddler says one word, model a full sentence back. This teaches grammar and new vocabulary naturally.
actionSteps: If they say dog, respond with yes, the big brown dog is running
Add one or two words to whatever they say
Do this naturally in conversation without asking them to repeat

title: Ask Questions and Wait for Responses (Late Infant)
description: Asking your toddler questions and then waiting patiently gives them practice forming thoughts into words.
actionSteps: Ask simple questions like where is the ball or do you want more
Wait at least 5 seconds for a response before repeating or answering yourself
Accept gestures, sounds, and attempts at words as valid responses

title: Name Body Parts Through Games (Late Infant)
description: Learning body part names builds vocabulary and body awareness. Games like Where is your nose make it fun.
actionSteps: Point to and name body parts during bath time and dressing
Play pointing games asking where is your nose, where are your toes
Sing Head Shoulders Knees and Toes with actions

title: Read Interactive Books with Flaps and Textures (Late Infant)
description: Interactive books engage toddlers and encourage participation in reading, building both language and fine motor skills.
actionSteps: Choose lift-the-flap and touch-and-feel books
Ask your child to find objects on the page or lift the flap
Let them help turn pages and point to pictures they recognize

title: Narrate Your Activities Together (Late Infant)
description: Describe what you and your toddler are doing throughout the day. This running commentary builds vocabulary and comprehension.
actionSteps: Describe actions as they happen like now we are washing the red apple
Use position words like in, on, under, and behind during play
Talk about what you see, hear, smell, and feel on walks

title: Support Bilingual Language Development (Late Infant)
description: If your family speaks more than one language, expose your child to both. Bilingualism is beneficial for brain development.
actionSteps: Use the one-parent-one-language approach or the home-language and community-language approach
Do not worry about mixing languages as this is normal and temporary
Read books and sing songs in both languages

title: Use Gestures and Signs to Support Language (Late Infant)
description: Baby signs and gestures reduce frustration and actually accelerate spoken language development.
actionSteps: Teach simple signs like more, all done, milk, and help
Always say the word while making the sign
Phase out signs naturally as spoken words replace them

title: Limit Screen Time to Protect Language Development (Late Infant)
description: Screen time does not teach language effectively. Live human interaction is far superior for language development.
actionSteps: Prioritize face-to-face interaction over educational videos
If using screens, co-view and talk about what you see together
Replace screen time with reading, singing, and conversation

title: Encourage Simple Problem-Solving (Late Infant)
description: Let your toddler work through challenges before helping. Figuring out how to fit a shape or open a container builds thinking skills.
actionSteps: Offer shape sorters, simple puzzles, and nesting cups
Wait and observe before offering help when they struggle
Give hints rather than solutions to encourage independent thinking

title: Introduce Sorting and Categorizing (Late Infant)
description: Sorting objects by color, shape, or size develops mathematical thinking and logical classification skills.
actionSteps: Sort laundry by color together
Group toys by type like all the animals or all the blocks
Use everyday items like different colored socks or utensils for sorting games

title: Play Hide and Seek with Objects (Late Infant)
description: Finding hidden objects at increasingly difficult levels develops memory, problem-solving, and persistence.
actionSteps: Start by hiding a toy under one of two cups and asking where it is
Progress to hiding objects in a room and giving verbal clues
Play simple hide and seek games where you hide and they find you

title: Introduce Simple Counting in Daily Life (Late Infant)
description: Count things naturally throughout the day. Stairs, snacks, toes, and blocks all become counting opportunities.
actionSteps: Count steps as you walk upstairs together
Count snack pieces as you give them
Touch and count objects rather than just reciting numbers

title: Support Understanding of Size and Space (Late Infant)
description: Concepts like big and small, in and out, up and down are foundational for math and spatial reasoning.
actionSteps: Use size words during play like the big ball and the little ball
Play with nesting cups and stacking rings that demonstrate size ordering
Use spatial language constantly like the ball went under the table

title: Encourage Cause-and-Effect Experimentation (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers love testing what happens when they do things. Support safe experimentation with cause and effect.
actionSteps: Provide water play with cups, funnels, and containers for pouring experiments
Let them press buttons, flip switches, and turn knobs on appropriate toys
Ask simple questions like what happens if you push it

title: Build Memory Through Repetition and Routine (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers learn through repetition. Reading the same book and playing the same game builds memory and comprehension.
actionSteps: Read favorite books repeatedly without impatience
Play the same games and sing the same songs often
Let them complete familiar phrases in songs and stories

title: Introduce Simple Matching Games (Late Infant)
description: Matching identical objects or pictures develops visual discrimination and memory, both important for later learning.
actionSteps: Play simple matching card games with just a few pairs
Match socks from the laundry basket together
Point out things that are the same and different during daily life

title: Facilitate Parallel Play Opportunities (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers play alongside but not with each other. This parallel play is normal and an important step toward cooperative play.
actionSteps: Arrange play dates where children play in the same space with similar toys
Provide duplicate toys to reduce conflicts
Narrate positive interactions like look, you are both building with blocks

title: Begin Teaching Sharing Gradually (Late Infant)
description: True sharing is difficult before age 3-4. Start with turn-taking and trading rather than expecting spontaneous generosity.
actionSteps: Model sharing by offering things to your child and saying I am sharing with you
Practice turn-taking with a timer for popular toys
Praise any voluntary sharing or turn-taking enthusiastically

title: Label Emotions in Social Situations (Late Infant)
description: Help your toddler understand their own and others' emotions by naming feelings as they occur in real situations.
actionSteps: Say you look frustrated that your friend took the toy
Point out other children's feelings like she is crying, she might be sad
Use picture books to identify and discuss emotions in characters

title: Guide Gentle Behavior with Other Children (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers may hit, push, or bite because they lack words for frustration. Teach gentle alternatives consistently.
actionSteps: Show them what gentle hands look like by demonstrating gentle touch
When they hit, calmly say hitting hurts, use gentle hands
Help them with words to say like I want a turn or that is mine

title: Practice Simple Social Scripts (Late Infant)
description: Teach basic social phrases like hi, bye, please, and thank you through modeling and gentle encouragement.
actionSteps: Model saying hi and bye to people and wave when baby does
Prompt but do not force saying please and thank you
Celebrate attempts at social niceties even if pronunciation is imperfect

title: Support Play with Older and Younger Children (Late Infant)
description: Mixed-age play benefits toddlers. Older children model advanced skills while younger ones practice nurturing.
actionSteps: Arrange supervised mixed-age play in safe environments
Guide older children to be gentle and patient with younger ones
Point out to your toddler what the older child is doing to encourage imitation

title: Help Your Toddler Cope with Social Frustration (Late Infant)
description: When a toy is taken or a playmate says no, your toddler needs help processing the big feelings that arise.
actionSteps: Acknowledge the feeling first with I know you are upset
Offer solutions like let us ask for a turn or let us find another toy
Stay close during play dates to mediate conflicts gently

title: Encourage Imitation of Prosocial Behavior (Late Infant)
description: Toddlers naturally imitate. When they see you being kind, helping, and comforting others, they begin to do the same.
actionSteps: Let your child see you help a neighbor or comfort a friend
Praise any prosocial behavior like giving a toy to a crying friend
Read books about characters who help, share, and show kindness

title: Establish a Consistent Bedtime Routine (Pre-Toddler)
description: A predictable 20-30 minute wind-down routine signals to your toddler that sleep is coming. Consistency is more important than the specific activities.
actionSteps: Choose 3-4 calming activities in the same order each night
Include a bath, pajamas, book reading, and a song or cuddle
Start the routine at the same time each evening

title: Navigate the Two-to-One Nap Transition (Pre-Toddler)
description: Between 12-18 months, most toddlers drop to one midday nap. Watch for signs like resisting the morning nap or taking very short naps.
actionSteps: Gradually push the morning nap later by 15-30 minutes
Offer an earlier bedtime during the transition period
Expect some fussy afternoons for 2-4 weeks during adjustment

title: Handle Bedtime Resistance with Calm Consistency (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers often test boundaries at bedtime by asking for extra drinks, stories, or hugs. Acknowledge their feelings but hold the boundary.
actionSteps: Offer one final choice before lights out (which book or which stuffed animal)
Use a calm firm voice to say goodnight and leave the room
If they get up, silently return them to bed without engaging

title: Ensure 11-14 Hours of Total Sleep Daily (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers aged 12-24 months need 11-14 hours of sleep including naps. Consistent insufficient sleep affects mood, learning, and growth.
actionSteps: Track total sleep including naps over a week to spot patterns
Adjust bedtime earlier if your toddler seems chronically overtired
Protect nap time as a non-negotiable part of the daily schedule

title: Create a Sleep-Friendly Toddler Room (Pre-Toddler)
description: As your child transitions from crib to toddler bed, ensure the room is safe and conducive to sleep.
actionSteps: Childproof the room so your toddler is safe if they get out of bed
Remove screens and stimulating toys from the sleep area
Use a dim nightlight if your child is afraid of the dark

title: Manage Separation Anxiety at Bedtime (Pre-Toddler)
description: Separation anxiety peaks around 8-18 months. Your child may cry when you leave the room at bedtime, which is developmentally normal.
actionSteps: Practice short separations during the day to build confidence
Create a brief consistent goodbye ritual at bedtime
Reassure your child you will be nearby but avoid prolonged check-ins

title: Eliminate Screens Before Bedtime (Pre-Toddler)
description: Screen exposure within 1-2 hours of bedtime delays melatonin production and makes it harder for toddlers to fall asleep.
actionSteps: Turn off all screens at least one hour before bedtime
Replace screen time with books, puzzles, or quiet play
Keep tablets and phones out of the bedroom entirely

title: Address Night Waking in Toddlers (Pre-Toddler)
description: Occasional night waking is normal but frequent waking may indicate overtiredness, hunger, or environmental disruption.
actionSteps: Keep the response brief and boring to avoid reinforcing waking
Check for teething, illness, or room temperature issues
Ensure your child is getting enough daytime calories and activity

title: Introduce Solids at Six Months with Iron-Rich Foods (Pre-Toddler)
description: At 6 months, start complementary foods alongside continued breastfeeding. Iron-rich foods are critical as iron stores from birth begin to deplete.
actionSteps: Start with iron-fortified cereal, pureed meat, or mashed lentils
Introduce one new single-ingredient food every 3-5 days
Continue breastfeeding alongside solids through 12 months and beyond

title: Progress Textures by 10 Months to Prevent Feeding Difficulties (Pre-Toddler)
description: WHO research shows that delaying lumpy foods past 10 months increases the risk of feeding difficulties later. Progress textures gradually.
actionSteps: Move from purees to mashed foods with soft lumps by 8-9 months
Offer safe finger foods like soft cooked vegetables and ripe fruit
By 10 months, include finely chopped family foods

title: Cut Foods Safely to Prevent Choking (Pre-Toddler)
description: Certain food shapes and sizes are choking hazards for young children. Always cut round foods lengthwise and avoid hard small items.
actionSteps: Quarter grapes and cherry tomatoes lengthwise
Cut hot dogs into thin strips, never round coins
Cook carrots and apples until soft before serving

title: Introduce Common Allergens from Six Months (Pre-Toddler)
description: Current evidence shows no benefit to delaying allergens. Introduce peanut, egg, dairy, wheat, fish, and soy from 6 months.
actionSteps: Start with thin peanut butter mixed with water or breast milk
Introduce one new allergen at a time and wait 3-5 days
Continue offering allergens regularly once introduced without reaction

title: Avoid Honey Before 12 Months (Pre-Toddler)
description: Honey can contain botulism spores that are dangerous for babies under one year. Their digestive system cannot neutralize the toxin.
actionSteps: Check ingredient labels for hidden honey in foods and drinks
Do not use honey as a sweetener in homemade baby food
After 12 months, honey is safe to introduce in small amounts

title: Practice Responsive Feeding with Eye Contact (Pre-Toddler)
description: Feed your toddler directly with patience. Make eye contact, go at their pace, and never force food. This builds healthy eating habits.
actionSteps: Sit face-to-face during meals and make eye contact
Offer food and wait for your child to open their mouth willingly
Stop feeding when they turn away or show disinterest

title: Offer 3-4 Meals Plus Snacks for Toddlers (Pre-Toddler)
description: Between 12-24 months, toddlers need 3-4 meals and 1-2 nutritious snacks daily. Small stomachs need frequent refueling.
actionSteps: Offer meals and snacks at regular times to establish rhythm
Include a variety of food groups at each meal
Keep snacks nutritious rather than using them as treats or pacifiers

title: Let Your Toddler Self-Feed to Build Independence (Pre-Toddler)
description: Messy self-feeding is an important developmental step. It builds fine motor skills and gives your child autonomy over eating.
actionSteps: Offer finger foods they can pick up independently
Provide a child-sized spoon and let them practice even if messy
Put a mat under the high chair and accept the mess as part of learning

title: Understand That Tantrums Are Developmentally Normal (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers have big feelings but limited vocabulary and impulse control. Tantrums are how they express frustration, not manipulation.
actionSteps: Stay calm and present during the tantrum without giving in or punishing
Acknowledge their feeling by saying something like you seem really frustrated
Wait for the storm to pass then offer comfort and move on

title: Offer Two Choices Instead of Saying No (Pre-Toddler)
description: Giving your toddler choices between two acceptable options reduces power struggles and builds decision-making skills.
actionSteps: Instead of do you want to get dressed ask red shirt or blue shirt
Offer choices you are comfortable with either outcome
Limit to two options to avoid overwhelming your toddler

title: Use Positive Language to Guide Behavior (Pre-Toddler)
description: Tell your toddler what to do rather than what not to do. Positive instructions are easier for young brains to process.
actionSteps: Say walk please instead of don't run
Say gentle hands instead of don't hit
Say food stays on the table instead of don't throw food

title: Stay Calm During Your Toddler's Big Emotions (Pre-Toddler)
description: Your toddler co-regulates through you. When you stay calm, you model emotional regulation and help them feel safe.
actionSteps: Take a deep breath before responding to challenging behavior
Use a low calm voice even when your child is screaming
Remove yourself briefly if you feel your own anger rising

title: Prepare Your Toddler for Transitions (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers struggle with sudden changes. Giving warnings before transitions reduces meltdowns.
actionSteps: Give a 5-minute and 2-minute warning before ending an activity
Use a timer or song to make the transition predictable
Describe what is coming next to help them mentally prepare

title: Catch Your Toddler Being Good (Pre-Toddler)
description: Attention is the most powerful motivator for toddlers. Praising positive behavior increases it more than punishing negative behavior.
actionSteps: Specifically name the good behavior you see
Say things like I love how you shared that toy with your sister
Give attention during calm moments not just during misbehavior

title: Set Simple Clear Boundaries Consistently (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers feel secure when they know the rules. Consistent boundaries with warm enforcement build cooperation over time.
actionSteps: Choose a few important non-negotiable rules and enforce them every time
Use short clear statements like we sit at the table to eat
Follow through calmly with the same consequence each time

title: Avoid Shaming or Harsh Discipline (Pre-Toddler)
description: Shaming damages self-esteem and does not teach better behavior. Gentle correction teaches while preserving your child's dignity.
actionSteps: Correct the behavior not the child by saying hitting hurts instead of you are bad
Avoid yelling or threatening as primary discipline tools
Use natural consequences when safe and appropriate

title: Childproof Your Home Before Your Baby Crawls (Pre-Toddler)
description: Childproofing should happen before your baby becomes mobile. Cover outlets, secure furniture, and lock cabinets with hazardous items.
actionSteps: Install outlet covers, cabinet locks, and furniture anchors
Gate the top and bottom of stairs
Get on hands and knees to identify hazards at your child's level

title: Lock Away All Medications and Cleaning Products (Pre-Toddler)
description: Poisoning is a leading cause of injury in toddlers. All medications and chemicals must be in locked cabinets, not just out of reach.
actionSteps: Use child-resistant locks on cabinets with medications and cleaners
Store items in original containers with labels intact
Save poison control number in your phone

title: Never Leave Your Child Alone Near Water (Pre-Toddler)
description: Drowning can happen in as little as one inch of water in under two minutes. Constant supervision near any water source is essential.
actionSteps: Stay within arm's reach during bath time, every single time
Empty buckets, kiddie pools, and containers immediately after use
Install pool fencing with self-closing gates if you have a pool

title: Secure Heavy Furniture and TVs to Walls (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers climb on everything. Unsecured furniture and televisions tip over, causing serious injuries and deaths every year.
actionSteps: Anchor bookshelves, dressers, and TV stands to wall studs
Place heavy items on lower shelves to lower center of gravity
Remove items from furniture tops that might tempt climbing

title: Teach Basic Water Safety Early (Pre-Toddler)
description: Even before swimming lessons, teach your toddler to never approach water without an adult and to wait for permission.
actionSteps: Teach your child to always ask an adult before going near water
Practice sitting on pool or tub edges, not standing
Consider infant survival swimming lessons starting at 6 months

title: Use Window Guards and Stops (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers can fall through screens. Install window guards or stops that prevent windows from opening more than 4 inches.
actionSteps: Install window guards on all windows above ground floor
Move furniture away from windows to prevent climbing access
Open windows from the top rather than the bottom when possible

title: Keep Button Batteries Away from Children (Pre-Toddler)
description: Button batteries can cause fatal internal burns if swallowed. They are found in remotes, toys, watches, and hearing aids.
actionSteps: Tape battery compartments shut on devices within reach
Dispose of used batteries immediately and securely
Seek emergency care immediately if you suspect battery ingestion

title: Apply Sunscreen and Protect from Sun Exposure (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddler skin is sensitive to UV damage. Use sunscreen, hats, and shade to prevent sunburn, which increases skin cancer risk later.
actionSteps: Apply broad-spectrum SPF 30+ sunscreen 15 minutes before going outside
Reapply every 2 hours and after water or sweating
Use wide-brimmed hats, sunglasses, and UPF clothing for extra protection

title: Ensure 180 Minutes of Physical Activity Daily (Pre-Toddler)
description: WHO recommends at least 3 hours of physical activity per day for toddlers, including active play, walking, and climbing.
actionSteps: Break activity into multiple sessions throughout the day
Include outdoor play whenever weather permits
Provide safe spaces for crawling, walking, climbing, and running

title: Limit Screen Time Appropriately for Age (Pre-Toddler)
description: No screen time before 12 months, and no more than 1 hour per day from ages 2-5. Screen time displaces active play and social interaction.
actionSteps: Avoid screens entirely for children under 12 months
For ages 1-2, choose high-quality educational content only if any
Co-view and discuss content rather than using screens as a babysitter

title: Encourage Problem-Solving Before Helping (Pre-Toddler)
description: When your toddler struggles with a task, wait and observe before stepping in. Productive struggle builds problem-solving skills.
actionSteps: Count to 10 internally before offering help
Offer hints or partial help rather than doing it for them
Celebrate their persistence regardless of the outcome

title: Create a Yes-Space for Safe Exploration (Pre-Toddler)
description: Designate an area where everything is safe for your toddler to explore freely. This reduces the need for constant no.
actionSteps: Childproof one room or area completely so everything is safe to touch
Rotate interesting and age-appropriate items in this space
Allow free exploration without hovering or restricting

title: Watch for Regression After Big Changes (Pre-Toddler)
description: Developmental regression (loss of previously acquired skills) after events like a new sibling or move is usually temporary.
actionSteps: Maintain routines as much as possible during transitions
Offer extra comfort and patience during regression periods
Contact your pediatrician if regression lasts more than a few weeks or seems unrelated to life changes

title: Encourage Imitation and Pretend Play (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers learn through imitation. Pretend play like feeding a doll or talking on a toy phone develops cognitive and social skills.
actionSteps: Provide simple pretend play props like toy phones, dolls, and play food
Join in their pretend play and follow their lead
Model everyday activities like cooking and cleaning for them to copy

title: Know When to Seek Developmental Evaluation (Pre-Toddler)
description: Trust your instincts as a parent. If something feels off about your child's development, early evaluation is always better than waiting.
actionSteps: Seek evaluation if your child is not walking by 18 months
Get assessed if there are no words by 16 months or no two-word phrases by 24 months
Do not let anyone dismiss your concerns with they will grow out of it without proper screening

title: Support Development Through Outdoor Nature Play (Pre-Toddler)
description: Nature provides rich sensory experiences that support development. Outdoor time improves motor skills, attention, and mood.
actionSteps: Visit parks, gardens, and natural areas regularly
Let your child explore grass, sand, water, leaves, and sticks
Name plants, animals, insects, and natural phenomena you encounter together

title: Begin Dental Care with the First Tooth (Pre-Toddler)
description: Start brushing as soon as the first tooth appears. Early dental care prevents cavities and establishes lifelong habits.
actionSteps: Use a smear of fluoride toothpaste on a soft infant toothbrush
Brush twice daily, especially before bed
Schedule the first dental visit by age 1 or within 6 months of the first tooth

title: Teach Hand Washing as a Habit (Pre-Toddler)
description: Hand washing is the best defense against illness. Make it a routine part of your toddler's day.
actionSteps: Wash hands before meals, after toileting, and after playing outside
Sing a 20-second song to ensure thorough washing
Use a step stool so your child can reach the sink independently

title: Provide Fluoride Through Tap Water (Pre-Toddler)
description: Fluoridated water strengthens developing teeth and prevents cavities. If your water is not fluoridated, ask your dentist about supplements.
actionSteps: Use tap water for drinking if it is fluoridated in your area
Limit juice to half a cup per day and avoid sugary beverages
Check with your dentist about fluoride varnish at regular visits

title: Keep Immunizations Up to Date Through Toddler Years (Pre-Toddler)
description: The vaccination schedule continues through toddlerhood. Timely boosters maintain protection against preventable diseases.
actionSteps: Follow the recommended 12, 15, and 18 month vaccine schedules
Keep your child's immunization record updated and accessible
Ask about any missed or delayed vaccines at every visit

title: Recognize Signs of Common Childhood Illnesses (Pre-Toddler)
description: Learn to identify ear infections, hand-foot-mouth disease, croup, and other common toddler illnesses to seek appropriate care.
actionSteps: Watch for ear pulling with fever as a sign of ear infection
Know that a barking cough at night may be croup
Call your doctor for persistent high fever, difficulty breathing, or signs of dehydration

title: Ensure Adequate Iron Intake After 12 Months (Pre-Toddler)
description: Iron deficiency is common in toddlers. After transitioning from formula or continued breastfeeding, ensure iron-rich food sources.
actionSteps: Offer iron-rich foods like lean meats, beans, fortified cereals, and spinach
Pair iron-rich foods with vitamin C sources for better absorption
Limit cow's milk to 16-24 ounces per day as excess milk decreases iron absorption

title: Establish a Regular Sleep Schedule for Immune Health (Pre-Toddler)
description: Consistent adequate sleep strengthens your toddler's immune system. Sleep-deprived children get sick more often.
actionSteps: Maintain a consistent bedtime and wake time
Ensure total sleep meets the 11-14 hour recommendation
Prioritize sleep during illness with earlier bedtimes

title: Schedule Regular Well-Child Visits Through Toddlerhood (Pre-Toddler)
description: Continue well-child visits at 12, 15, 18, and 24 months. These visits track growth, development, and catch emerging health issues.
actionSteps: Do not skip visits even if your child seems healthy
Bring a written list of developmental questions or concerns
Ask about lead screening, vision, and hearing at appropriate ages

title: Follow Your Child's Lead in Play (Pre-Toddler)
description: When you let your toddler direct play, they feel valued and in control. This builds confidence and deepens your connection.
actionSteps: Sit at their level and wait for them to invite you into play
Narrate what they are doing instead of directing the activity
Resist the urge to teach or correct during child-led play time

title: Praise New Skill Attempts Enthusiastically (Pre-Toddler)
description: When your toddler tries something new, your encouragement matters more than their success. Celebrate the attempt.
actionSteps: Cheer for attempts even when they do not succeed
Use specific praise like you tried so hard to stack those blocks
Avoid only praising results or comparing to other children

title: Read Together Every Day (Pre-Toddler)
description: Daily reading is the single most impactful activity for your child's development. It builds vocabulary, comprehension, and closeness.
actionSteps: Read at least one book together every day making it a non-negotiable routine
Let your child choose the book even if it is the same one again
Ask questions about the pictures and let them turn the pages

title: Create Special One-on-One Time (Pre-Toddler)
description: Dedicated time with each child, even 10-15 minutes of focused attention, strengthens your relationship more than hours of distracted togetherness.
actionSteps: Set aside at least 10 minutes daily of phone-free child-focused time
Let your child choose the activity during this special time
Call it by a special name like our special time to make it feel important

title: Use Physical Affection Generously (Pre-Toddler)
description: Hugs, cuddles, and physical closeness release oxytocin in both parent and child, strengthening the bond and reducing stress.
actionSteps: Offer hugs, lap sitting, and cuddles throughout the day
Respect when your child pulls away and do not force physical affection
Create a goodnight ritual that includes physical connection like a hug and a kiss

title: Let Your Toddler Help with Household Tasks (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers love to imitate grown-up activities. Including them in chores builds competence, connection, and a sense of belonging.
actionSteps: Give them a small cloth to wipe surfaces while you clean
Let them help stir, pour, or wash vegetables during cooking
Thank them for their help and tell them they are an important part of the family

title: Expand Your Toddler's Words with Conversation (Pre-Toddler)
description: When your toddler says a word, expand it into a full sentence. This builds language while showing you listen and care about what they say.
actionSteps: If they say ball, respond with yes, it is a big red ball
Add new information to their observations like the dog is running fast
Pause and give them time to respond even if it takes a while

title: Be Present During Transitions and Hard Moments (Pre-Toddler)
description: Your calm presence during difficult moments like goodbyes, doctor visits, or frustrations teaches your child they can count on you.
actionSteps: Stay close and offer comfort during hard transitions like daycare drop-off
Acknowledge their feelings with I know this is hard
Avoid sneaking away or dismissing their distress

title: Support Cruising and Early Walking Safely (Pre-Toddler)
description: When your baby starts pulling up and cruising along furniture, ensure the environment supports safe practice.
actionSteps: Arrange sturdy furniture for cruising with no sharp corners
Clear the floor of tripping hazards
Offer push toys that provide stability for walking practice

title: Encourage Climbing in Safe Environments (Pre-Toddler)
description: Toddlers are natural climbers. Rather than stopping all climbing, provide safe opportunities to develop this important skill.
actionSteps: Set up safe climbing opportunities with cushions, low steps, or toddler climbers
Supervise closely and stay nearby to spot them
